Sanju Samson: Samson came through Kerala cricket as a rare top-order wicketkeeper-batter with timing, range, and early leadership responsibility.
His Rajasthan Royals rise made him one of the earliest Indian keeper-batters trusted for top-order IPL intent.
Made his India T20I debut in 2015, beginning a long stop-start national-team story shaped by competition for keeper-batter roles.
Rajasthan made him captain, turning him from a high-upside batter into a franchise decision-maker.
Scored his first ODI hundred against South Africa, a major proof point that his international ceiling could translate beyond IPL cameos.
For CSK, Samson changes the roster geometry: top-order batting, keeping cover, and captaincy experience in one player.
Dewald Brevis: Brevis emerged as a high-ceiling South African batting prospect with a style often compared to AB de Villiers.
The 2022 U19 World Cup made him globally visible through volume scoring and 360-degree strokeplay.
Mumbai Indians and global leagues accelerated his development before he became a regular senior international name.
Made his South Africa T20I debut, moving from prospect label to senior international pathway.
His career is built around boundary access, spin-hitting confidence, and the ability to change tempo quickly.
For CSK, Brevis offers a youthful overseas power option who can be protected by a veteran-heavy structure.
Matthew Short: Short came through Australian domestic cricket as a batting all-rounder capable of opening and bowling off-spin.
His Big Bash form made him a franchise target because he could score quickly and add matchup overs.
Made his Australia white-ball debut after sustained domestic T20 returns.
His best BBL seasons combined top-order run volume with useful overs, turning him into a genuine all-round option.
Short’s value is that he can open, float, or cover off-spin depending on team balance.
For CSK, he profiles as a tactical overseas all-rounder rather than a single-role batter.
Akeal Hosein: Hosein came through West Indies cricket as a left-arm spinner with control, new-ball courage, and lower-order batting value.
Made his West Indies white-ball debut and quickly became trusted in powerplay and middle-over phases.
Built a global T20 profile around economy, angles, and matchup bowling to right-hand-heavy lineups.
His West Indies role has often been about controlling scoring rate while still creating wicket pressure.
The key value is phase flexibility: he can bowl early, through the middle, or protect a matchup.
For CSK, Hosein gives spin depth and overseas control if conditions reward grip.
Matt Henry: Henry came through New Zealand domestic cricket as a seam bowler with bounce, movement, and relentless Test-match discipline.
Made his New Zealand debut and became a regular squad option across formats.
Was part of New Zealand’s 2019 World Cup final campaign, with new-ball spells central to their attack.
His best seasons have come when seam movement and hard lengths made him a red-ball and ODI threat.
Henry’s value is early wickets: forcing top-order errors before innings shape settles.
For CSK, Henry gives an overseas seam option with international tournament experience.
David Miller: Miller emerged from South African cricket as a left-handed power batter built for finishing and middle-order acceleration.
Made his South Africa debut in 2010 and became a long-running white-ball presence.
His unbeaten 101 for Kings XI Punjab became one of the iconic early IPL finishing innings.
Played a major finishing role in Gujarat Titans’ 2022 IPL title campaign.
His international value has been calm finishing, boundary hitting, and experience in knockout pressure.
For Delhi, Miller supplies late-innings power and a veteran overseas left-hand option.
Pathum Nissanka: Nissanka came through Sri Lanka as a technically strong top-order batter trusted for stability.
Made a Test hundred on debut against West Indies, immediately validating his international temperament.
Became a key Sri Lanka white-ball opener through repeatable starts and controlled strokeplay.
Scored Sri Lanka’s first ODI double century, a major individual landmark in national cricket history.
His strongest years have paired ODI volume with T20 adaptability at the top.
For Delhi, Nissanka offers a composed overseas top-order option if the XI needs control over volatility.
Mitchell Starc: Starc developed as a rare left-arm quick with pace, swing, yorkers, and lower-order hitting.
Made his Australia debut in 2010 and grew into one of the defining white-ball fast bowlers of his era.
Was Player of the Tournament as Australia won the 2015 ODI World Cup.
Led the wicket charts at the 2019 World Cup, reinforcing his tournament-bowling reputation.
Produced major knockout spells for KKR in 2024, showing why left-arm pace is a premium playoff weapon.
For Delhi, Starc is a new-ball and death-over strike option with proven tournament pedigree.
Lungi Ngidi: Ngidi came through South African cricket as a tall fast bowler with bounce and seam movement.
Made his international debut and quickly became part of South Africa’s fast-bowling pool.
Announced himself in Tests with wicket-taking spells against India.
Was part of Chennai’s 2018 IPL title-winning setup, giving him early franchise-winning experience.
His strongest value is hard-length bowling with wicket threat in the middle overs.
For Delhi, Ngidi gives overseas pace depth and proven IPL familiarity.
Shubman Gill: Gill came through Punjab cricket and India U19 as a polished top-order batter with elite timing.
Was Player of the Tournament as India won the 2018 U19 World Cup, making him a national future-star marker.
Made his India debut and gradually moved from prospect to all-format top-order option.
His 91 at the Gabba helped India chase history and became a career-defining Test innings.
Scored 890 runs in IPL 2023, winning the Orange Cap and carrying Gujarat’s batting to another final.
Took over Gujarat captaincy, adding tactical and leadership weight to his batting role.
Jos Buttler: Buttler emerged from English cricket as a rare keeper-batter with elite white-ball finishing and opening range.
Made his England debut in 2011 and became central to England’s limited-overs transformation.
Was England’s wicketkeeper in the 2019 ODI World Cup final win, including the decisive Super Over run-out.
Scored 863 runs for Rajasthan in IPL 2022, winning the Orange Cap with a run of hundreds.
Succeeded Eoin Morgan as England’s white-ball captain and led the 2022 T20 World Cup-winning side.
For Gujarat, Buttler offers elite opening power, keeping cover, and knockout experience.
Kagiso Rabada: Rabada came through South African cricket as an unusually complete fast bowler: pace, bounce, yorkers, and aggression.
Made his international debut as a teenager and quickly became a multi-format strike bowler.
Took a hat-trick on ODI debut, one of the clearest early signs of his wicket-taking ceiling.
Became one of the leading Test fast bowlers in the world through strike rate and hostility.
Won the IPL Purple Cap with Delhi Capitals, proving he could dominate franchise cricket too.
For Gujarat, Rabada gives a premium overseas strike option in both powerplay and death phases.
Rashid Khan: Rashid emerged as Afghanistan’s global cricket symbol: fast-arm leg-spin, googlies, and fearless lower-order hitting.
Made his Afghanistan debut as a teenager and rapidly became the face of their T20 rise.
Joined Sunrisers Hyderabad and immediately became one of the IPL’s hardest bowlers to attack.
Built a worldwide league career by combining economy, wickets, fielding, and finishing cameos.
Played a central role in Gujarat Titans’ 2022 IPL title-winning campaign.
For Gujarat, Rashid remains a tactical centerpiece: middle-over control, late hitting, and leadership aura.
Cameron Green: Green emerged from Western Australia as a rare tall seam-bowling all-rounder with top-order batting range.
Made his Australia debut in 2020 and was quickly valued for balancing XIs with both batting and seam overs.
Developed through Test cricket as a tall fourth-seamer who could also hold a top-six batting role.
Scored a maiden IPL hundred for Mumbai Indians, proving he could dominate as a top-order T20 batter.
His IPL value is premium because few overseas players combine top-order batting, seam bowling, and fielding reach.
For KKR, Green gives a title-contending XI a flexible overseas all-rounder route.
Rachin Ravindra: Ravindra came through New Zealand cricket as a left-handed batter and left-arm spin all-rounder.
Made his New Zealand debut in 2021, initially valued for multi-skill balance.
The 2023 ODI World Cup transformed his profile through top-order hundreds and fearless batting.
Added red-ball weight with major Test runs, showing his game was not just white-ball momentum.
His IPL arrival gave him experience in Indian conditions and franchise pressure.
For KKR, Ravindra offers left-hand batting, spin cover, and a high-upside overseas role.
Sunil Narine: Narine emerged from Trinidad as a mystery spinner with a hard-to-read action and elite T20 control.
Made his West Indies debut and quickly became one of the most difficult white-ball spinners to attack.
Was part of West Indies’ 2012 T20 World Cup-winning side, validating his global spin impact.
Was central to KKR’s 2012 and 2014 IPL titles through spin control and wicket pressure.
Reinvented himself as a powerplay hitter, culminating in an MVP-level 2024 IPL season.
For KKR, Narine remains franchise DNA: spin, opening batting disruption, and tactical unpredictability.
Rishabh Pant: Pant came through Delhi cricket as a left-handed wicketkeeper-batter with rare power and improvisation.
Made his India debut in 2017 and quickly became a high-risk, high-reward batting option.
Scored Test hundreds in England and Australia, unusual markers for an Indian keeper-batter.
His unbeaten 89 at the Gabba sealed one of India’s greatest Test series wins.
Returned from a serious accident to resume elite cricket, adding comeback weight to his profile.
For LSG, Pant is captain, keeper, middle-order disruptor, and emotional center of the squad.
Nicholas Pooran: Pooran came through West Indies cricket as a left-handed keeper-batter with elite ball-striking.
Made his West Indies debut and became part of the next generation of Caribbean T20 power.
Built global value through finishing, spin hitting, and middle-order acceleration across leagues.
Captained West Indies in white-ball cricket, adding leadership experience to his batting profile.
His best IPL seasons have been defined by high strike-rate middle-order impact for LSG.
For LSG, Pooran is the overseas batting engine who can flip an innings in two overs.
Mitchell Marsh: Marsh came through a famous Australian cricket family as a seam-bowling all-rounder with top-order power.
Made his Australia debut in 2011 and spent years moving between formats and roles.
Made an unbeaten 77 in the 2021 T20 World Cup final, one of Australia’s biggest T20 title innings.
Returned to Test prominence with aggressive middle-order runs during a major Australia cycle.
His best seasons combine top-three batting power with useful seam overs when fit.
For LSG, Marsh offers a high-ceiling overseas all-rounder who can bat in the powerplay or middle overs.
Rohit Sharma: Rohit came through Mumbai cricket as a gifted timer before becoming one of India’s defining white-ball openers.
Made his India debut in 2007 and was part of the first T20 World Cup-winning squad.
Moving to opener changed his career, unlocking ODI double hundreds and long-innings dominance.
Scored 264 against Sri Lanka, the highest individual score in ODI history.
Led Mumbai Indians through a title dynasty, becoming one of the IPL’s most successful captains.
Captained India to the 2024 T20 World Cup title, adding a global trophy to his leadership arc.
Suryakumar Yadav: Suryakumar built his career through Mumbai domestic cricket and years of IPL consistency before India selection.
Made his India debut in 2021 and immediately brought 360-degree T20 strokeplay to the international stage.
Rose to the top of the T20I batting rankings through outrageous boundary options and consistency.
Scored multiple T20I hundreds, turning innovation into repeatable output.
Was part of India’s 2024 T20 World Cup win, including the defining boundary catch in the final.
For MI, he remains the middle-over tempo controller who can make conventional fields look obsolete.
Trent Boult: Boult emerged as a left-arm swing bowler with one of the most threatening new-ball skill sets in world cricket.
Made his New Zealand debut and became a long-term partner in their world-class pace attack.
Was central to New Zealand’s 2015 World Cup final campaign and led the tournament wicket charts jointly.
Played another major role as New Zealand reached the 2019 World Cup final.
Was a key new-ball wicket-taker in Mumbai Indians’ 2020 IPL title season.
For MI, Boult gives familiar left-arm powerplay threat and playoff-tested experience.
Quinton de Kock: De Kock came through South Africa as an attacking left-handed wicketkeeper-opener.
Made his international debut and quickly became an all-format top-order player.
Built a reputation for fast ODI hundreds and taking games away early.
Was part of Mumbai Indians’ dominant title years, giving them left-hand opening balance.
Produced major ODI World Cup runs in 2023, reinforcing his big-tournament quality.
For MI, de Kock offers a proven overseas keeper-opener route if the XI needs experience and left-hand tempo.
Shreyas Iyer: Iyer came through Mumbai cricket as an attacking top-order player strong against spin.
Made his India debut in 2017 and grew into a middle-order option across formats.
Led Delhi Capitals to a new competitive phase, including the 2020 IPL final.
Scored major middle-order runs in India’s 2023 ODI World Cup campaign.
Captained KKR to the 2024 IPL title, making leadership a central part of his IPL identity.
For Punjab, Iyer is captain, spin-hitter, middle-order organiser, and tactical anchor.
Arshdeep Singh: Arshdeep came through Punjab as a left-arm seamer with yorker control and death-overs temperament.
Made his India T20I debut and quickly became trusted in high-pressure overs.
His value comes from being usable at both ends of a T20 innings.
Was part of India’s 2024 T20 World Cup-winning attack and finished among the tournament’s leading wicket-takers.
Punjab retained him as a domestic pace cornerstone because left-arm death bowling is scarce.
For Punjab, Arshdeep is the bowling leader who sets powerplay and death-over plans.
Yuzvendra Chahal: Chahal came through as a leg-spinner with chess-like planning and excellent white-ball deception.
Made his India white-ball debut and became part of the Kuldeep-Chahal spin phase.
Took 6 for 25 against England, one of India’s great T20I bowling performances.
Became RCB’s key middle-over wicket-taker through years of high-risk home conditions.
Won the IPL Purple Cap with Rajasthan Royals, underlining his franchise wicket-taking durability.
For Punjab, Chahal gives middle-over wicket threat and matchup control against right-hand-heavy lineups.
Ravindra Jadeja: Jadeja came through Saurashtra as a left-arm spin all-rounder and elite fielder.
Made his India debut in 2009 and gradually became a three-dimensional all-format cricketer.
Was a major part of India’s 2013 Champions Trophy win, including tournament-leading bowling impact.
Became one of the world’s best Test all-rounders through control, batting growth, and fielding.
Hit the title-winning boundary for CSK in the 2023 IPL final, a defining franchise moment.
For Rajasthan, Jadeja adds senior all-round control, elite fielding, and title-winning experience.
Yashasvi Jaiswal: Jaiswal’s story runs from difficult Mumbai beginnings to elite domestic and U19 run-making.
Was Player of the Tournament at the 2020 U19 World Cup, making him an India future-batting marker.
Exploded for Rajasthan Royals in IPL 2023 with high-tempo opening and a record-fast fifty.
Made a Test hundred on India debut in the West Indies, immediately validating red-ball quality.
Produced huge Test runs against England, including double hundreds, showing elite long-format ceiling.
For Rajasthan, Jaiswal is the franchise’s high-ceiling Indian opener and long-term batting face.
Heinrich Klaasen: Klaasen emerged as a wicketkeeper-batter with unusual power against spin.
Made his South Africa debut and quickly showed value as a middle-order disruptor.
Built a global T20 reputation as one of the most destructive spin hitters in the world.
Scored a brilliant IPL hundred for SRH, proving his ability to dominate even in struggling team contexts.
Was central to SRH’s ultra-aggressive batting identity alongside Head and Abhishek.
For SRH, Klaasen is the middle-order engine who punishes spin and protects momentum.
Ishan Kishan: Kishan came through Jharkhand as a left-handed wicketkeeper-batter with top-order power.
Captained India at the 2016 U19 World Cup, marking him as an early leadership prospect.
Made his India debut in 2021 and immediately brought attacking left-hand options to the top order.
Scored the fastest ODI double hundred, a career-defining statement of ceiling and tempo.
Built IPL value through Mumbai Indians as a keeper-opener and middle-order option.
For SRH, Kishan adds left-hand aggression and keeping depth to an already explosive top order.
Sarfaraz Khan: Sarfaraz came through Mumbai cricket as a heavy-scoring middle-order batter after early age-group visibility.
Entered the IPL as a teenager with RCB, showing improvisation and confidence before his first-class game fully matured.
Built one of the strongest modern Ranji Trophy scoring records, repeatedly forcing India selection debate through huge first-class returns.
Made his Test debut against England at Rajkot and scored twin fifties, proving he could handle the emotional weight of the long wait.
Converted his first Test hundred into 150 against New Zealand in Bengaluru, the specific international moment his timeline must carry.
For CSK, Sarfaraz is a spin-strong Indian batting option whose red-ball patience can be adapted into middle-over control.
Rajat Patidar: Patidar came through Madhya Pradesh cricket as a technically clean top-order batter with strong spin-hitting range.
His unbeaten 112 in the IPL Eliminator against Lucknow became one of RCB’s great knockout innings.
Was part of Madhya Pradesh’s Ranji Trophy title season, adding first-class credibility to the IPL breakout.
Made his India ODI debut against South Africa after sustained domestic and IPL performances.
Made his Test debut against England at Visakhapatnam, a late-bloomer reward after years of domestic work.
For RCB, Patidar is captain and top-order stabilizer, carrying the post-title leadership story in a red-blue-gold environment.
Devdutt Padikkal: Padikkal rose through Karnataka cricket as a tall left-handed opener with timing and off-side fluency.
Made an immediate IPL impression for RCB with top-order runs and composure in his first full season.
Scored an unbeaten IPL hundred for RCB, proving his ability to turn starts into statement innings.
Made his India T20I debut in Sri Lanka as part of a young touring group.
Made his Test debut against England at Dharamsala and scored a half-century, a major red-ball checkpoint.
For RCB, Padikkal gives left-hand top-order depth and a familiar franchise connection.
Phil Salt: Salt’s cricket identity blends a Barbados childhood with English county development and fearless top-order hitting.
Made his England debut as a white-ball opener/keeper option built for powerplay aggression.
Hit his maiden T20I hundred against West Indies, becoming one of England’s rare T20I centurions.
Followed with another T20I hundred, becoming the first England men’s batter with two T20I centuries.
Played a major powerplay role in KKR’s 2024 IPL title season before international duty interrupted the playoff stretch.
For RCB, Salt is a high-tempo keeper-opener who can make the first six overs feel like a chase-breaker.
Jitesh Sharma: Jitesh came through Vidarbha as an attacking wicketkeeper-batter suited to finishing and pace hitting.
Made his IPL reputation with Punjab through fearless middle-order cameos and wicketkeeping flexibility.
Made his India T20I debut after the IPL showed he could attack from ball one.
His international chances centered on the specialist keeper-finisher role, a hard slot in India’s white-ball depth chart.
Was part of RCB’s title-era squad structure, bringing lower-order acceleration and keeping depth.
For RCB, Jitesh is the domestic keeper-finisher who protects the XI if Salt is used differently.
Tim David: David’s path is unusual: born in Singapore, internationally capped there, then moving into Australia’s T20 setup.
Played T20Is for Singapore before his global franchise hitting made Australia selection possible.
Built IPL value as a death-overs hitter for Mumbai Indians, specializing in short high-impact innings.
Made his Australia T20I debut as a late-overs power option.
His best seasons are measured less by aggregates than by strike-rate, six-hitting, and final-four-over threat.
For RCB, David is the designated late-innings accelerator who can turn 18 balls into match separation.
Romario Shepherd: Shepherd came through Guyana as a seam-bowling all-rounder with lower-order hitting power.
Made his West Indies debut and moved into the T20 all-rounder pool.
His T20 value comes from heavy hitting at the back end and useful seam overs.
In IPL roles, he is selected for condensed impact rather than long spells or long innings.
His international role sits in the Caribbean power-all-rounder tradition.
For RCB, Shepherd gives overseas finishing cover and seam balance if matchups demand power.
Jacob Bethell: Bethell moved through England’s age-group system as a left-handed batter and left-arm spin all-rounder.
England’s U19 World Cup run made him visible as a high-upside modern all-rounder.
Made his England international debut in 2024, moving from prospect to senior white-ball option.
Earned England Test exposure during a rapid rise, showing how highly the setup rated his ceiling.
His value comes from left-hand batting, spin cover, fielding, and age-profile upside.
For RCB, Bethell is the developmental overseas all-rounder with room to become more than a matchup pick.
Krunal Pandya: Krunal came through Baroda as a left-arm spin all-rounder with a direct, combative batting style.
Became part of Mumbai Indians’ title machine, offering spin, fielding, and lower-order left-hand hitting.
Made his India T20I debut as a spin-bowling all-rounder.
Scored a rapid fifty on ODI debut against England, an emotional innings after family loss.
His best seasons are built on utility: two-phase spin, matchup batting, and senior game sense.
For RCB, Krunal gives domestic all-round balance and a left-arm spin option in pressure phases.
Bhuvneshwar Kumar: Bhuvneshwar came through Uttar Pradesh as a swing bowler with unusually refined new-ball control.
Made his India debut with immediate swing impact, including early wickets against Pakistan.
Was part of India’s 2013 Champions Trophy-winning squad.
Won back-to-back IPL Purple Caps for Sunrisers Hyderabad, a rare bowling consistency achievement.
Led the wickets column in SRH’s 2016 IPL title run, connecting individual peak to team silverware.
For RCB, Bhuvneshwar brings powerplay swing, death-over experience, and calm senior bowling intelligence.
Josh Hazlewood: Hazlewood developed as a tall, accurate Australian fast bowler with relentless hard lengths.
Made his Australia debut as a teenager and grew into a three-format pace option.
Was part of Australia’s 2015 ODI World Cup-winning squad.
Became one of Australia’s Test attack pillars through accuracy, bounce, and seam movement.
Played a key role as Australia won the 2021 T20 World Cup.
For RCB, Hazlewood is the overseas control quick who can bowl powerplay pressure without losing shape.
Harshal Patel: Harshal built his profile through domestic cricket as a clever medium-fast bowler and lower-order hitter.
Won the IPL Purple Cap with 32 wickets for RCB, tying the then record for most wickets in an IPL season.
Made his India T20I debut after the slower-ball and death-over craft translated into elite IPL output.
His value sits in pace-off control, cutters, and tactical deception when batters are forced to attack.
For SRH, Harshal is an Indian death-over option who can change pace on batting-friendly surfaces.
Abhishek Sharma: Abhishek came through Punjab and India U19 as a left-handed batter with useful left-arm spin.
Was part of India’s 2018 U19 World Cup-winning squad, connecting him to a strong generation.
His 2024 IPL season transformed his profile through fearless powerplay hitting alongside Travis Head.
Made his India T20I debut after the IPL breakout demanded national attention.
Scored an early T20I hundred, proving the powerplay style could transfer to international cricket.
For SRH, Abhishek is the Indian half of their left-handed powerplay assault and a sixth-bowling option.
Nitish Kumar Reddy: Nitish came through Andhra cricket as a seam-bowling all-rounder with middle-order batting confidence.
The 2024 IPL season brought him into national focus through batting maturity and useful seam overs.
Made his India debut after the IPL proved he could handle pressure and role flexibility.
Scored a maiden Test hundred in Australia, a major all-rounder credibility marker early in his career.
His best seasons will be judged by whether batting progress and bowling volume rise together.
For SRH, Nitish gives domestic all-round balance without using an overseas slot.
Kamindu Mendis: Kamindu came through Sri Lanka as a rare ambidextrous spinner and left-handed batter.
Made his Sri Lanka debut with curiosity around his two-arm spin and all-round profile.
His 2024 Test run made him more than a novelty, establishing him as a serious international batter.
Reached early Test hundreds at unusual speed, turning his red-ball output into a global storyline.
His value is multi-layered: batting depth, spin variation, fielding, and matchup surprise.
For SRH, Kamindu is a tactical overseas all-rounder who can stretch team combinations.
Liam Livingstone: Livingstone emerged from Lancashire as a high-ceiling hitter with both off-spin and leg-spin options.
Made his England debut as a white-ball power option.
Scored a 42-ball T20I hundred against Pakistan, one of England’s great power-hitting displays.
Was part of England’s 2022 T20 World Cup-winning squad.
His best seasons are built on six-hitting, spin flexibility, and the threat of sudden acceleration.
For SRH, Livingstone adds a middle-order power layer to an already aggressive batting identity.
Gerald Coetzee: Coetzee came through South African cricket as a hostile fast bowler with short-ball energy and lower-order hitting.
Made his South Africa debut and quickly moved into their World Cup plans.
Produced a strong 2023 ODI World Cup, becoming South Africa’s leading wicket-taker in the tournament.
Entered IPL cricket as an overseas pace option with aggression and impact-ball potential.
His value is hostility: hard lengths, bouncers, and wicket-taking energy rather than containment alone.
For SRH, Coetzee gives pace depth behind Cummins and a different type of middle-over threat.
Rinku Singh: Rinku’s story came through Uttar Pradesh cricket and years of squad grinding before national recognition.
Hit five consecutive sixes off Yash Dayal to win a match for KKR, instantly becoming an IPL folklore figure.
Made his India T20I debut after the finishing role became impossible to ignore.
His India role grew because he was not only explosive; he was repeatably calm in late chases.
Remained in India’s T20 World Cup conversation as one of the clearest specialist finishers.
For KKR, Rinku is emotional core and left-handed finishing insurance.
Rovman Powell: Powell came through Jamaican cricket as a powerful middle-order hitter and athletic fielder.
Made his West Indies debut and became part of their modern T20 power pool.
Scored an ODI hundred for West Indies, showing he was not only a cameo hitter.
Captained West Indies in T20Is, adding leadership responsibility to the power-hitting role.
His best seasons are built around matchup hitting, late-over sixes, and pressure catching.
For KKR, Powell is overseas middle-order power and leadership cover.
Finn Allen: Allen emerged as a fearless New Zealand top-order batter with immediate boundary intent.
Made his New Zealand debut as a T20 opener designed to maximize the powerplay.
Scored a T20I hundred against Scotland, proving his international ceiling.
Made 137 against Pakistan in a T20I, one of New Zealand’s biggest short-format innings.
His role is clear: accept risk early and force fielding sides out of their default plan.
For KKR, Allen is an overseas opening option if they want maximum first-six-over aggression.
Tim Seifert: Seifert came through Northern Districts as a wicketkeeper-batter with fast hands and top-order T20 range.
Made his New Zealand debut as part of their white-ball keeping depth.
Made 84 against India in Wellington, one of the innings that defined his early international reputation.
Moved through T20 leagues as a flexible keeper-opener or middle-order option.
His best usage comes when he is asked to attack pace early rather than rebuild conservatively.
For KKR, Seifert gives overseas wicketkeeping depth and tempo insurance.
Varun Chakaravarthy: Varun’s path was unconventional, moving from architecture and tennis-ball cricket into professional mystery spin.
Became a central KKR bowler through variations, defensive control, and middle-over wickets.
Made his India T20I debut and was picked for the 2021 T20 World Cup squad.
Played a major role in KKR’s 2024 IPL title run with middle-over control and wicket pressure.
Kept himself in India T20 conversations through sustained IPL output.
For KKR, Varun remains a tactical spin pillar alongside Narine.
Umran Malik: Umran emerged from Jammu and Kashmir cricket as one of India’s fastest raw pace prospects.
His IPL speed for SRH made him a national talking point and earned fast-track attention.
Made his India debut after IPL spells regularly crossed high pace thresholds.
His value is not subtle: high pace, hard lengths, and the possibility of wickets through speed.
The career question is control: turning raw pace into repeatable pressure under T20 fields.
For KKR, Umran is a high-ceiling pace option who changes the energy of an innings.
Navdeep Saini: Saini rose from Haryana roots into Delhi cricket as a skiddy fast bowler with pace.
Made his India T20I debut and immediately showed wicket-taking pace.
Played across formats for India, including Test cricket in Australia.
Was part of India’s injury-hit Australia tour, a series remembered for depth and resilience.
His value is as a domestic quick with international experience and short-burst pace.
For KKR, Saini gives Indian pace depth behind the primary overseas strike options.
Washington Sundar: Washington came through Tamil Nadu cricket as a disciplined off-spinner with top-order batting ability.
Made his India T20I debut as a teenager, valued for powerplay off-spin.
Played a major role in India’s Nidahas Trophy win through control and composure.
Made a crucial 62 and took wickets on Test debut at the Gabba, one of India’s great depth stories.
His best seasons combine off-spin economy, batting depth, and matchup flexibility.
For Gujarat, Washington gives domestic all-round balance and powerplay spin options.
Mohammad Siraj: Siraj came through Hyderabad cricket as a relentless fast bowler with seam movement and emotional intensity.
Made his India debut in T20Is before growing into a Test and ODI strike bowler.
Led India’s attack at the Gabba in only his third Test, taking a five-wicket haul.
Took 6 for 21 against Sri Lanka in the Asia Cup final, one of India’s great ODI new-ball spells.
Was part of India’s dominant 2023 ODI World Cup pace unit.
For Gujarat, Siraj is the Indian new-ball spearhead with wicket-taking volatility.
Kuldeep Yadav: Kuldeep came through as a rare Indian left-arm wrist-spinner with drift, dip, and wicket-taking ambition.
Made his Test debut against Australia at Dharamsala and immediately gave India a different spin profile.
Took multiple ODI hat-tricks, showing his ability to change games in clusters.
Rebuilt his action and confidence to become central again in India’s white-ball plans.
Was part of India’s 2024 T20 World Cup-winning squad, used as an attacking middle-over wrist-spinner.
For Delhi, Kuldeep is the middle-over wicket engine who forces batters to take risks.
Mukesh Kumar: Mukesh came through Bengal cricket through long domestic spells and red-ball consistency.
Made his India Test debut in the West Indies, a late-earned reward for domestic seam work.
Played across formats for India, moving from red-ball control into white-ball roles.
His T20 value comes from yorkers, cutters, and calm execution at the end.
Delhi used him as an Indian pace option capable of both new-ball and death-over work.
For Delhi, Mukesh is domestic seam reliability in a squad with overseas strike options.
T. Natarajan: Natarajan rose from Tamil Nadu cricket with a signature yorker and a powerful underdog story.
His IPL 2020 death bowling made him a national story and earned India selection.
Made his India debut across formats on the Australia tour, a rare rapid multi-format entry.
Played the Gabba Test in India’s famous series win, adding red-ball weight to a white-ball specialist image.
His best seasons are defined by death-over trust and left-arm angle.
For Delhi, Natarajan gives Indian left-arm death bowling, one of the scarcest T20 roles.
Karun Nair: Karun came through Karnataka cricket as a technically organised top-order batter in a strong domestic system.
Made his India Test debut in 2016 after domestic volume and IPL exposure made him a middle-order option.
Scored 303 not out against England in Chennai, becoming only the second Indian after Virender Sehwag to make a Test triple century.
After falling out of the India side, his career became a persistence story through Ranji, county, and franchise opportunities.
His IPL value is experience: an Indian batter who has opened, anchored, and handled spin-heavy middle overs.
For Delhi, Karun is a depth batter whose triple-hundred ceiling still gives his profile historical weight.
Prithvi Shaw: Shaw came through Mumbai cricket as an attacking opener with school-record fame and elite age-group scoring.
Captained India to the 2018 U19 World Cup title, the same tournament that pushed Gill into the national future frame.
Made a century on Test debut against West Indies, immediately showing his international ceiling.
His strongest IPL windows came when Delhi used him as a pure powerplay tempo-setter.
His later career has been about fitness, consistency, and rebuilding trust around a very high batting ceiling.
For Delhi, Shaw is a volatility-and-upside opener: if rhythm returns, the first six overs change fast.
Nitish Rana: Rana came through Delhi cricket as a left-handed batter who could also offer part-time off-spin.
Built his IPL reputation through Mumbai Indians and then KKR as a spin-hitting top/middle-order option.
Made his India debut on the Sri Lanka tour, earning international recognition after IPL consistency.
Captained KKR in 2023, adding leadership to a profile previously defined by batting tempo.
His best seasons are when he controls matchups against spin and stabilizes innings without losing tempo.
For Delhi, Rana gives Indian left-hand batting depth and a senior IPL voice.
Sai Sudharsan: Sai Sudharsan came through Tamil Nadu cricket as a left-handed top-order batter with calm tempo and strong domestic returns.
Made 96 in the 2023 IPL final for Gujarat Titans, an innings that announced his big-stage temperament.
Made his India ODI debut against South Africa and started with composed top-order runs.
Kept building as Gujarat’s Indian batting pillar, combining anchor qualities with boundary access.
His career arc includes red-ball and county exposure, pointing toward more than a T20-only profile.
For Gujarat, Sai is the left-handed batting stabilizer around whom innings can be structured.
Aiden Markram: Markram came through as captain of South Africa’s 2014 U19 World Cup-winning side.
Made his Test debut and quickly scored early hundreds, suggesting a long-term top-order future.
Captained Sunrisers Eastern Cape to the inaugural SA20 title, strengthening his franchise leadership profile.
Captained SRH in the IPL, connecting his South African leadership success to the Hyderabad setup.
His T20 value comes from top-order batting, off-spin matchups, and leadership calm.
For LSG, Markram gives overseas batting control and a secondary leadership voice around Pant.
Matthew Breetzke: Breetzke came through South African cricket as a top-order wicketkeeper-batter with strong domestic white-ball returns.
Made his South Africa T20I debut as part of their next wave of batting options.
Scored 150 on ODI debut, a record-setting innings that immediately gave his international profile substance.
Built franchise credibility through SA20 and domestic performances before wider league demand grew.
His value is top-order batting with keeping flexibility and a willingness to play tempo cricket.
For LSG, Breetzke is overseas batting depth behind a stacked keeper-batter group.
Avesh Khan: Avesh came through Madhya Pradesh and India U19 as a hit-the-deck fast bowler.
His 2021 IPL season with Delhi made him one of the tournament’s leading Indian wicket-takers.
Made his India T20I debut after IPL output created a clear fast-bowling case.
His strongest T20 spells use hard lengths, pace, and defensive fields to force miscues.
Remained in India white-ball squad conversations as a pace-depth option.
For LSG, Avesh is a domestic fast bowler who can take high-pressure overs without using an overseas slot.
Anrich Nortje: Nortje emerged as one of South Africa’s fastest bowlers, built around speed and hard lengths.
Made his South Africa debut and quickly entered their World Cup and Test pace plans.
His 2020 IPL season with Delhi, alongside Rabada, made him one of the fastest and most feared quicks in the league.
His identity is speed: pushing batters back and changing scoring options through pace alone.
Injuries have shaped his availability, but when fit he changes the physical tone of an attack.
For LSG, Nortje is the overseas pace enforcer who gives Pant a pure speed option.
Mohammed Shami: Shami rose through Bengal cricket as a seam bowler with one of the cleanest wrist positions in world cricket.
Made his India debut and quickly became a wicket-taking new-ball and reverse-swing option.
Took a World Cup hat-trick against Afghanistan, reinforcing his tournament wicket threat.
Won the IPL Purple Cap for Gujarat Titans with new-ball wickets and relentless seam control.
Was the leading wicket-taker at the 2023 ODI World Cup, including devastating knockout spells.
For LSG, Shami gives senior Indian pace leadership and proven tournament strike value.
Marcus Stoinis: Stoinis developed as a powerful Australian batting all-rounder with seam-bowling utility.
Made his Australia debut and became a flexible white-ball squad option.
Scored 146 not out against New Zealand while almost pulling off a solo chase, the innings that defined his ceiling.
Was part of Australia’s 2021 T20 World Cup-winning squad.
Became a core LSG overseas all-rounder through middle-order hitting and matchup overs.
For Punjab, Stoinis brings senior overseas power and a seam option in one slot.
Marco Jansen: Jansen came through South Africa as a very tall left-arm fast-bowling all-rounder.
Made his Test debut against India and immediately looked dangerous with bounce and angle.
His batting improved enough to make him a genuine all-round option rather than a tailender.
Was part of South Africa’s 2023 World Cup campaign as a new-ball and lower-order contributor.
His value is rare: left-arm pace, height, powerplay wickets, and batting depth.
For Punjab, Jansen gives balance and a point-of-difference angle in the pace group.
Azmatullah Omarzai: Omarzai came through Afghanistan cricket as a seam-bowling all-rounder with top/middle-order batting growth.
Made his Afghanistan debut and quickly became part of their all-rounder core.
Produced important all-round moments in Afghanistan’s strong 2023 ODI World Cup campaign.
His all-round output earned global recognition as one of Afghanistan’s key modern players.
His value is balance: seam overs, batting flexibility, and big-match temperament.
For Punjab, Omarzai is an overseas all-rounder who can lengthen both batting and bowling.
Lockie Ferguson: Ferguson emerged from New Zealand cricket as an express quick with a heavy short ball.
Made his New Zealand debut and moved quickly into white-ball strike plans.
Was one of New Zealand’s leading wicket-takers during their 2019 World Cup final campaign.
Built IPL value through short bursts of speed and middle-over wicket threat.
Produced a rare four-maiden T20I spell at the 2024 T20 World Cup, a statistical outlier for pace control.
For Punjab, Ferguson gives an overseas pace shock option when surfaces reward speed.
Dhruv Jurel: Jurel came through Uttar Pradesh and India U19 cricket as a composed wicketkeeper-batter.
Earned IPL attention with calm lower-order finishing for Rajasthan Royals.
Made his India Test debut against England during a series that introduced several new Indian players.
His 90 at Ranchi against England was a pressure innings that turned him from prospect into serious Test option.
His value is composure: keeping skill, lower-order batting, and calm under scoreboard pressure.
For Rajasthan, Jurel is domestic keeper-batter depth with genuine India-level credibility.
Shimron Hetmyer: Hetmyer came through West Indies cricket as a bold left-handed batter with elite shot power.
Captained West Indies to the 2016 U19 World Cup title, a major early leadership achievement.
Made his West Indies debut and became a high-upside middle-order option.
Scored multiple ODI hundreds, showing he had more than cameo power.
For Rajasthan, he became a left-handed finisher trusted to attack spin and pace late.
Hetmyer remains RR’s overseas finishing force and matchup disruptor.
Dasun Shanaka: Shanaka came through Sri Lankan cricket as a seam-bowling all-rounder and lower-order power hitter.
Made his Sri Lanka debut and moved through the white-ball all-rounder pathway.
Captained Sri Lanka through a rebuilding phase, becoming a central dressing-room figure.
Led Sri Lanka to the 2022 Asia Cup title, one of the country’s major modern white-ball achievements.
His best innings are late-order momentum swings rather than long accumulation.
For Rajasthan, Shanaka offers overseas leadership, finishing, and seam-bowling cover.
Jofra Archer: Archer’s route took him from Barbados to Sussex and then England, bringing extreme pace and rare composure.
Made his England debut in 2019 and was fast-tracked into their World Cup plans.
Bowled the Super Over as England won the 2019 ODI World Cup, a defining pressure moment.
Made an immediate Ashes impact with pace and hostility, including the spell to Steve Smith at Lord’s.
Injuries have interrupted his career, making every return a question of workload and explosive value.
For Rajasthan, Archer is the premium pace weapon if fit: powerplay threat, middle-over fear, death-over ceiling.
Nandre Burger: Burger emerged as a left-arm quick with pace, bounce, and wicket-taking aggression.
Made his South Africa debut across formats in 2023, quickly showing white-ball and red-ball promise.
His early international spells against India gave him credibility beyond domestic cricket.
Joined Rajasthan as a left-arm pace option who could change powerplay matchups.
His value is angle and aggression: attacking right-handers from over the wicket and rushing batters.
For Rajasthan, Burger gives left-arm pace depth behind bigger-name quicks.
